expanded_description: A comprehensive exploration of single displacement (replacement) reactions, where one element replaces another element in a compound, guided by the activity series which predicts which metals can displace others based on their relative reactivity.

core_concept.principle: Single displacement reactions follow the pattern $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$, where a more reactive element (A) replaces a less reactive element (B) in a compound, determined by the activity series of metals.
core_concept.explanation: In single displacement reactions, an element reacts with a compound and takes the place of another element in that compound. The reaction only occurs if the free element is more reactive than the element it's replacing. The activity series ranks metals by reactivity—metals higher on the series can displace metals lower on the series from their compounds. This pattern is essential for predicting reaction outcomes, understanding corrosion, and industrial metal extraction processes.

image.alt: Diagram showing element A replacing element B in compound BC
image.caption: Single displacement pattern: $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$; more reactive element replaces less reactive element.

sections.introduction: Single displacement reactions, also called single replacement reactions, are fundamental chemical processes where one element takes the place of another in a compound. These reactions are governed by the relative reactivity of elements, which determines whether a reaction will occur.

key_concept.definition: A single displacement reaction is a chemical reaction in which one element replaces another element in a compound, following the general pattern $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$, where A is a more reactive element than B.
key_concept.context: Single displacement reactions are redox reactions (oxidation-reduction) because electrons are transferred. The more reactive element is oxidized (loses electrons), while the less reactive element is reduced (gains electrons). The activity series predicts which reactions will occur spontaneously.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron transfer in single displacement reaction
image.caption: Electron transfer: more reactive metal loses electrons (oxidized), less reactive metal gains electrons (reduced).

classifications.value: More reactive metal replaces less reactive metal in compound
characteristics: Free metal + metal compound → new metal compound + free metal
Visible metal deposition or color change
classifications.behavior: More reactive metal loses electrons (oxidized) and goes into solution; less reactive metal gains electrons (reduced) and comes out of solution as solid metal.

examples.explanation: $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{CuSO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$. Zinc is more reactive than copper (higher on activity series). Zinc dissolves, copper metal precipitates as red-brown solid. Solution changes from blue ($\text{Cu}^{2+}$) to colorless ($\text{Zn}^{2+}$).

image.alt: Zinc metal in copper sulfate solution showing copper deposition
image.caption: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$: zinc displaces copper, red copper metal forms.

examples.explanation: $\text{Fe}(s) + \text{CuSO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$. Iron is more reactive than copper. Iron nail becomes coated with copper. Solution changes from blue to pale green ($\text{Fe}^{2+}$).

image.alt: Iron nail in copper sulfate showing copper coating
image.caption: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$: iron displaces copper, copper coats iron.

examples.explanation: $2\text{Al}(s) + 3\text{CuSO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3(aq) + 3\text{Cu}(s)$. Aluminum is much more reactive than copper. Vigorous reaction, copper precipitates. Aluminum oxide coating must be removed for reaction to occur.

image.alt: Aluminum in copper sulfate solution
image.caption: $2\text{Al} + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{Cu}$: aluminum vigorously displaces copper.

classifications.value: Metal replaces hydrogen in acid or water
characteristics: Metal + water → metal hydroxide + hydrogen gas
Only metals above H in activity series react
classifications.behavior: Metal loses electrons to $\text{H}^+$ ions, producing $\text{H}_2$ gas. Reaction vigor depends on metal reactivity: K/Na react explosively with water, Mg reacts with steam, Zn reacts with acids, Cu doesn't react.

examples.explanation: $\text{Mg}(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)\uparrow$. Magnesium is above H in activity series. Hydrogen gas bubbles form rapidly. Test with burning splint confirms $\text{H}_2$ (pop sound).

image.alt: Magnesium reacting with hydrochloric acid producing bubbles
image.caption: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$: metal displaces H from acid.

examples.explanation: $\text{Zn}(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)\uparrow$. Zinc is above H in activity series. Moderate reaction rate, steady $\text{H}_2$ production. Common laboratory method for generating hydrogen.

image.alt: Zinc reacting with acid producing hydrogen gas
image.caption: $\text{Zn} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$: laboratory hydrogen production.

examples.explanation: $2\text{Na}(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH}(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)\uparrow + \text{energy}$. Sodium is very reactive (top of activity series). Violent reaction, $\text{H}_2$ may ignite. Sodium stored under oil to prevent reaction.

image.alt: Sodium reacting violently with water
image.caption: $2\text{Na} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2$: very reactive metal displaces H from water.

classifications.value: More reactive halogen replaces less reactive halogen in compound
characteristics: Halogen + metal halide → new metal halide + halogen
Reactivity decreases down Group 17: F > Cl > Br > I
classifications.behavior: More reactive halogen (higher on Group 17) gains electrons from halide ion, displacing less reactive halogen. Fluorine is most reactive, iodine is least reactive among common halogens.

examples.explanation: $\text{Cl}_2(aq) + 2\text{NaBr}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{Br}_2(aq)$. Chlorine is more reactive than bromine. Solution changes from colorless to orange-brown ($\text{Br}_2$). Used to extract bromine from seawater.

image.alt: Chlorine water added to sodium bromide showing color change
image.caption: $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{NaBr} \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{Br}_2$: chlorine displaces bromine, orange color appears.

examples.explanation: $\text{Cl}_2(aq) + 2\text{KI}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{KCl}(aq) + \text{I}_2(aq)$. Chlorine is more reactive than iodine. Solution changes to brown ($\text{I}_2$). Add starch to confirm: blue-black color indicates iodine.

image.alt: Chlorine displacing iodine with starch test
image.caption: $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{KI} \rightarrow 2\text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$: chlorine displaces iodine, starch turns blue-black.

examples.explanation: $\text{Br}_2(aq) + 2\text{KI}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{KBr}(aq) + \text{I}_2(aq)$. Bromine is more reactive than iodine. Orange-brown $\text{Br}_2$ changes to brown $\text{I}_2$. Iodine less reactive than both Cl and Br.

image.alt: Bromine displacing iodine from potassium iodide
image.caption: $\text{Br}_2 + 2\text{KI} \rightarrow 2\text{KBr} + \text{I}_2$: bromine displaces iodine.

principles.statement: The activity series ranks metals by reactivity; metals higher on the series can displace metals lower on the series from their compounds, but not vice versa.
principles.application: Predicts if single displacement will occur: Zn can replace Cu (Zn > Cu), but Cu cannot replace Zn (Cu < Zn). Activity series: Li > K > Ca > Na > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Ni > Sn > Pb > H > Cu > Ag > Au.
principles.example: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$ occurs (Zn > Cu in activity series). $\text{Cu} + \text{ZnSO}_4 \rightarrow$ no reaction (Cu < Zn, cannot replace). Activity series is essential prediction tool.

image.alt: Activity series chart showing metal reactivity order with H reference
image.caption: Activity series: metals above H react with acids; metals above another metal can displace it.

principles.statement: Single displacement reactions are oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions: the displacing element is oxidized (loses electrons), the displaced element is reduced (gains electrons).
principles.application: Identifies electron transfer: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$ (oxidation), $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$ (reduction). More reactive metal has greater tendency to lose electrons.
principles.example: $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$. Zn loses 2 electrons (oxidized, reducing agent). $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ gains 2 electrons (reduced, oxidizing agent). Electron transfer drives reaction.

image.alt: Electron transfer diagram for single displacement reaction
image.caption: Redox in displacement: Zn oxidized (loses e⁻), Cu²⁺ reduced (gains e⁻).

principles.statement: To predict if single displacement occurs: (1) Identify free element and element in compound, (2) Check activity series, (3) If free element is higher (more reactive), reaction occurs; if lower, no reaction.
principles.application: Step-by-step prediction: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4$ → Fe > Cu? Yes → reaction occurs. $\text{Cu} + \text{FeSO}_4$ → Cu > Fe? No → no reaction. Always check activity series first.
principles.example: $\text{Mg} + \text{ZnSO}_4$ → Mg > Zn? Yes (Mg higher) → $\text{MgSO}_4 + \text{Zn}$. $\text{Ag} + \text{HCl}$ → Ag > H? No (Ag lower) → no reaction. Activity series determines outcome.

image.alt: Flowchart for predicting single displacement reactions
image.caption: Prediction flowchart: check activity series → compare positions → predict reaction.

components.definition: Single displacement reaction components and patterns
components.equation: $\text{Single Displacement Pattern: } A + BC \rightarrow AC + B \quad \text{(where A > B in activity series)}$
components.interpretation: Free element (A) replaces element (B) in compound only if A is more reactive than B. Activity series determines feasibility. Redox reaction with electron transfer.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Single Displacement Reactions
worked_examples.problem: Predict if the following reactions occur. If yes, write balanced equation. If no, write 'no reaction': (a) $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow$, (b) $\text{Cu} + \text{ZnSO}_4 \rightarrow$, (c) $\text{Mg} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow$, (d) $\text{Ag} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow$.

step_by_step.calculation: Check activity series: Zn > Cu? Yes (Zn higher). Reaction occurs. Products: $\text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$. Balance: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$ (already balanced).
Check activity series: Cu > Zn? No (Cu lower). Reaction does NOT occur. Answer: no reaction.
Check activity series: Mg > H? Yes (Mg above H). Reaction occurs. Products: $\text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. Balance: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$.
Check activity series: Ag > H? No (Ag below H). Reaction does NOT occur. Answer: no reaction.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$ (Zn > Cu, reaction occurs). (b) no reaction (Cu < Zn, cannot displace). (c) $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$ (Mg > H, reaction occurs). (d) no reaction (Ag < H, cannot displace). Key: always check activity series first to predict if reaction occurs.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using activity series to predict single displacement reaction occurrence and writing balanced equations

worked_examples.title: Real-World Application: Metal Extraction
worked_examples.problem: Iron can be extracted from copper(II) sulfate waste using scrap iron: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$. (a) Why does this reaction occur? (b) What mass of copper can be recovered from 100 g $\text{CuSO}_4$? (c) Why is this economically beneficial?

step_by_step.calculation: Fe is higher than Cu on activity series (Fe > Cu). Iron is more reactive, can displace copper from solution. Redox reaction: Fe oxidized to $\text{Fe}^{2+}$, $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ reduced to Cu.

Molar mass $\text{CuSO}_4 = 63.55 + 32.07 + 4(16.00) = 159.62$ g/mol. Molar mass Cu = 63.55 g/mol. Ratio: 1 mol $\text{CuSO}_4$ → 1 mol Cu. 100 g $\text{CuSO}_4 \times \frac{1 \text{ mol}}{159.62 \text{ g}} \times \frac{63.55 \text{ g Cu}}{1 \text{ mol}} = 39.8$ g Cu.

Scrap iron is cheap and abundant. Copper is valuable ($\sim$\$8/kg). Recovering copper from waste streams generates revenue, reduces waste disposal costs, and conserves copper resources. Environmentally friendly recycling.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Reaction occurs because Fe > Cu on activity series; iron is more reactive and displaces copper. (b) 100 g $\text{CuSO}_4$ produces 39.8 g Cu (1:1 molar ratio). (c) Economically beneficial: cheap scrap iron recovers valuable copper from waste, generates revenue, reduces environmental impact. Single displacement enables metal recovery and recycling.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying single displacement reaction principles to industrial metal recovery and economic considerations

sections.introduction: The activity series is a fundamental tool in chemistry that ranks metals by their reactivity. Understanding this series enables prediction of single displacement reactions, corrosion behavior, and appropriate metal selection for various applications.

key_concept.definition: The activity series is a list of metals (and hydrogen) arranged in order of decreasing reactivity, where metals higher on the list can displace metals lower on the list from their compounds in single displacement reactions.
key_concept.context: The activity series is based on experimental observations of which metals react with water, acids, and metal salt solutions. It reflects the tendency of metals to lose electrons (oxidation potential). Metals at the top lose electrons most easily; metals at the bottom are most stable.

image.alt: Complete activity series chart with reactivity zones marked
image.caption: Activity series: Li (most reactive) to Au (least reactive); H reference point for acid reactions.

classifications.value: Li, K, Ca, Na—react vigorously with cold water
characteristics: Must be stored under oil to prevent reaction with air/moisture
Never found as free elements in nature
classifications.behavior: These metals lose electrons so easily they react with water at room temperature, producing $\text{H}_2$ gas and metal hydroxide. Too reactive for structural uses; used in specialized applications (batteries, chemical synthesis).

examples.explanation: $2\text{Na}(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH}(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)\uparrow + \text{energy}$. Violent reaction, $\text{H}_2$ may ignite. Sodium stored under mineral oil. Used in sodium vapor lamps, chemical synthesis.

image.alt: Sodium reacting violently with water
image.caption: Very reactive metals: Na reacts explosively with water, stored under oil.

examples.explanation: $2\text{K}(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow 2\text{KOH}(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)\uparrow + \text{energy}$. Even more violent than Na. Purple flame from K combustion. Used in fertilizers (KCl), specialized applications.

image.alt: Potassium reacting with water showing purple flame
image.caption: K more reactive than Na: violent reaction with purple flame.

classifications.value: Mg, Al, Zn, Fe—react with acids and steam
characteristics: React with acids to produce $\text{H}_2$ gas
classifications.behavior: These metals are reactive enough to displace H from acids and displace less reactive metals from solutions. Some form protective oxide coatings that slow further reaction (Al, Zn). Widely used in construction, manufacturing, galvanization.

examples.explanation: $\text{Mg}(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)\uparrow$. Vigorous reaction, rapid $\text{H}_2$ production. Mg burns with bright white light. Used in alloys, flares, fireworks.

image.alt: Magnesium reacting with acid producing bubbles
image.caption: Reactive metals: Mg reacts vigorously with acids, produces $\text{H}_2$.

examples.explanation: Zn coats steel to prevent rusting. Zn is more reactive than Fe, so Zn corrodes first (sacrificial protection). $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$ protects Fe from oxidation. Galvanized steel used in roofing, fencing.

image.alt: Galvanized steel showing zinc coating
image.caption: Zinc galvanization: Zn sacrificially protects Fe from corrosion.

examples.explanation: Al forms protective $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ layer that prevents further reaction. Despite being reactive, Al appears unreactive due to oxide coating. Used in aircraft, construction, packaging. Anodizing thickens protective layer.

image.alt: Aluminum oxide protective layer on aluminum surface
image.caption: Aluminum: reactive but protected by oxide layer, widely used structurally.

classifications.type: Less Reactive Metals (Lower Middle of Series)
classifications.value: Ni, Sn, Pb—react slowly with acids, don't react with water
characteristics: Don't react with water or steam
Can displace H and metals below them
classifications.behavior: These metals are moderately reactive. They can displace H from acids (slowly) and displace less reactive metals from solutions. Used in specialized applications where moderate reactivity is advantageous.

examples.explanation: Pb used in car batteries: $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Pb is reactive enough for electrochemical reactions but stable enough for long-term use. Rechargeable battery technology.

image.alt: Lead-acid car battery
image.caption: Lead-acid battery: Pb reactivity enables rechargeable electrochemical reactions.

examples.explanation: Sn used in solder (Sn-Pb alloy) for electronics. Sn is reactive enough to bond with metals but stable enough for long-term connections. Lead-free solder uses Sn-Ag-Cu alloys. Essential for electronics manufacturing.

image.alt: Tin solder on electronic circuit board
image.caption: Tin soldering: moderate reactivity enables metal bonding in electronics.

classifications.value: Cu, Ag, Au—don't react with acids or water
characteristics: Don't react with acids (except oxidizing acids)
Found as free elements in nature
Used for jewelry, coins, electrical wiring
classifications.behavior: These metals are very stable and don't easily lose electrons. They don't displace H from acids and are displaced by most other metals. Their stability makes them valuable for jewelry, currency, and applications requiring corrosion resistance.

examples.explanation: Cu used for electrical wiring because it doesn't corrode easily (low reactivity) and conducts electricity well. Cu doesn't react with water or dilute acids. Second only to Ag in conductivity. Essential for electrical infrastructure.

image.alt: Copper electrical wiring
image.caption: Copper wiring: low reactivity + high conductivity = ideal for electrical use.

examples.explanation: Au is least reactive metal (bottom of activity series). Doesn't tarnish, corrode, or react with acids (except aqua regia). Found as free element in nature. Valued for jewelry, coins, electronics. Stability = value.

image.alt: Gold jewelry and coins
image.caption: Gold: least reactive metal, doesn't tarnish, found as free element.

examples.explanation: Ag is very unreactive but reacts slowly with $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ in air to form black $\text{Ag}_2\text{S}$ (tarnish). $2\text{Ag} + \text{H}_2\text{S} + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ag}_2\text{S} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Still much less reactive than most metals. Used in jewelry, electronics, photography.

image.alt: Tarnished silver showing black Ag2S
image.caption: Silver tarnish: Ag reacts slowly with $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, but still very unreactive overall.

principles.statement: Hydrogen serves as a key reference point in the activity series: metals above H react with acids to produce $\text{H}_2$ gas; metals below H don't react with acids.
principles.application: Predicts acid reactions: Mg, Zn, Fe (above H) react with HCl to produce $\text{H}_2$. Cu, Ag, Au (below H) don't react with HCl. Quick test for metal reactivity relative to H.
principles.example: $\text{Zn} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$ (Zn > H, reaction occurs). $\text{Cu} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow$ no reaction (Cu < H, no reaction). H is the dividing line for acid reactivity.

image.alt: Activity series showing H reference point with metals above and below
image.caption: H reference: metals above react with acids; metals below don't react with acids.

principles.statement: Metal reactivity correlates with corrosion tendency: more reactive metals corrode more easily; less reactive metals are more corrosion-resistant.
principles.application: Explains why Fe rusts (reactive), Cu develops patina (moderately reactive), Au doesn't corrode (unreactive). Guides material selection: Au for corrosion resistance, Zn for sacrificial protection.
principles.example: Fe rusts readily (Fe + $\text{O}_2$ + $\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$). Au doesn't corrode (too stable). Zn protects Fe by corroding first (sacrificial anode). Activity series predicts corrosion behavior.

image.alt: Comparison of corrosion on different metals based on activity
image.caption: Corrosion tendency: more reactive metals corrode more easily; less reactive resist corrosion.

principles.statement: Metal extraction methods depend on position in activity series: very reactive metals require electrolysis; moderately reactive metals can be reduced with carbon; unreactive metals found as free elements.
principles.application: Na, K, Ca, Mg, Al: extracted by electrolysis (too reactive for chemical reduction). Zn, Fe, Sn, Pb: extracted by reduction with carbon (coke). Cu, Ag, Au: found as free elements or easily extracted.
principles.example: Al extraction: $2\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{electricity}} 4\text{Al} + 3\text{O}_2$ (electrolysis, energy-intensive). Fe extraction: $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}$ (carbon reduction). Au: found as free metal, minimal processing.

image.alt: Chart showing extraction methods based on activity series position
image.caption: Extraction methods: electrolysis (top), carbon reduction (middle), free element (bottom).

components.equation: $\text{Activity Series (decreasing reactivity): Li > K > Ca > Na > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Ni > Sn > Pb > H > Cu > Ag > Au}$
components.interpretation: Metals higher can displace metals lower. Metals above H react with acids. Top metals react with water. Bottom metals found as free elements. Use series to predict single displacement reactions.

worked_examples.title: Using Activity Series to Predict Reactions
worked_examples.problem: Use the activity series to predict if these reactions occur. If yes, write balanced equation. If no, write 'no reaction': (a) $\text{Al} + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow$, (b) $\text{Cu} + \text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow$, (c) $\text{Au} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow$, (d) $\text{Ca} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow$.

step_by_step.calculation: Check activity series: Al > Fe? Yes (Al higher). Reaction occurs (thermite reaction). Products: $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Fe}$. Balance: $2\text{Al} + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Fe}$.

Check activity series: Cu > Ag? Yes (Cu higher). Reaction occurs. Products: $\text{Cu}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + \text{Ag}$. Balance: $\text{Cu} + 2\text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Cu}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{Ag}$.
Check activity series: Au > H? No (Au much lower). Reaction does NOT occur. Answer: no reaction. Au is very unreactive.

Check activity series: Ca is very reactive (top of series). Reacts with cold water. Products: $\text{Ca}(\text{OH})_2 + \text{H}_2$. Balance: $\text{Ca} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{OH})_2 + \text{H}_2$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $2\text{Al} + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Fe}$ (Al > Fe, thermite reaction). (b) $\text{Cu} + 2\text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Cu}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{Ag}$ (Cu > Ag). (c) no reaction (Au < H, too unreactive). (d) $\text{Ca} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{OH})_2 + \text{H}_2$ (Ca very reactive, reacts with water). Activity series predicts all outcomes.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using activity series to predict single displacement reaction occurrence and writing balanced equations

worked_examples.title: Activity Series and Material Selection
worked_examples.problem: You need to select a metal for: (a) outdoor fencing (corrosion resistance needed), (b) sacrificial anode for ship hull protection, (c) electrical contacts (no corrosion). Use activity series to justify your choices.

step_by_step.calculation: Need corrosion resistance → less reactive metal. Options: Cu (expensive), Al (forms protective oxide, moderate cost), Fe (rusts, needs protection). Best choice: Al (protective $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ layer) or galvanized steel (Zn coating protects Fe).

Need MORE reactive metal than Fe (ship hull). Must corrode preferentially. Options: Zn, Mg, Al. Best choice: Zn (common, effective, cost-effective) or Mg (more reactive, for saltwater). Zn > Fe on activity series.

Need no corrosion + good conductivity → very unreactive metal. Options: Au (best, expensive), Ag (excellent, tarnishes), Cu (good, oxidizes). Best choice: Au for critical contacts (no corrosion), Cu for general use (cost-effective).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Outdoor fencing: Al (protective oxide layer) or galvanized steel (Zn protects Fe). (b) Sacrificial anode: Zn or Mg (more reactive than Fe, corrodes preferentially). (c) Electrical contacts: Au (best, no corrosion) or Cu (cost-effective). Activity series guides material selection based on reactivity and corrosion resistance.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying activity series knowledge to real-world material selection and corrosion prevention

sections.section_title: Writing and Balancing Single Displacement Equations
sections.introduction: Successfully writing single displacement equations requires predicting products correctly, determining if the reaction occurs, and balancing the equation. This skill is essential for understanding reaction stoichiometry and predicting reaction outcomes.

key_concept.definition: Writing single displacement equations involves: (1) identifying the free element and compound, (2) checking activity series to confirm reaction occurs, (3) predicting products by swapping elements, (4) writing correct formulas for products, (5) balancing the equation.
key_concept.context: Product formulas must be written correctly using ionic charges (for ionic compounds) or covalent naming rules. Balancing ensures Law of Conservation of Mass is satisfied. State symbols indicate physical states of reactants and products.

image.alt: Step-by-step flowchart for writing single displacement equations
image.caption: Equation writing steps: identify → check activity series → predict products → write formulas → balance.

principles.statement: In single displacement, the free element replaces the element in the compound: metal replaces metal (or H), halogen replaces halogen. Product formulas determined by charge balance (ionic) or covalent rules.
principles.application: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4$: Zn replaces Cu → $\text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$. $\text{Mg} + \text{HCl}$: Mg replaces H → $\text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{NaBr}$: Cl replaces Br → $\text{NaCl} + \text{Br}_2$.
principles.example: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuCl}_2 \rightarrow$? Fe replaces Cu. Products: $\text{FeCl}_2 + \text{Cu}$ (Fe forms $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ commonly). Balance: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuCl}_2 \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + \text{Cu}$ (already balanced).

image.alt: Diagram showing element replacement in single displacement
image.caption: Product prediction: free element replaces element in compound; write correct product formulas.

principles.statement: Product ionic compound formulas must be written using correct ionic charges: balance cation and anion charges to zero. Use common oxidation states for transition metals.
principles.application: Fe commonly forms $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ or $\text{Fe}^{3+}$. Cu commonly forms $\text{Cu}^{+}$ or $\text{Cu}^{2+}$. Use context or common states: Fe + acid → $\text{Fe}^{2+}$, Fe + strong oxidizer → $\text{Fe}^{3+}$.

image.alt: Examples of correct ionic formula writing for displacement products
image.caption: Ionic formulas: balance charges to zero; use common oxidation states for transition metals.

principles.statement: Balance single displacement equations by: (1) write correct product formulas first, (2) count atoms of each element, (3) adjust coefficients to balance, (4) verify all atoms balance. Never change subscripts.
principles.application: Start with most complex compound. Balance metals first, then nonmetals, then H and O last. Check diatomic elements ($\text{H}_2$, $\text{O}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$, etc.) are written correctly.
principles.example: $\text{Al} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow$ Products: $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{Cu}$. Balance Al: $2\text{Al} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{Cu}$. Balance $\text{SO}_4$: $2\text{Al} + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{Cu}$. Balance Cu: $2\text{Al} + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{Cu}$.

image.alt: Step-by-step balancing of single displacement equation
image.caption: Balancing strategy: write products first, then balance atoms with coefficients.

components.equation: $\text{Equation Writing: } A + BC \xrightarrow{\text{check activity}} AC + B \quad \text{(balance with coefficients)}$
components.interpretation: Free element (A) replaces element (B) in compound only if A > B in activity series. Write correct product formulas using charge balance. Balance with coefficients, never change subscripts.

worked_examples.title: Writing and Balancing Single Displacement Equations
worked_examples.problem: Write and balance equations for: (a) magnesium + hydrochloric acid, (b) aluminum + copper(II) sulfate, (c) chlorine + potassium bromide, (d) iron + silver nitrate.

step_by_step.calculation: Reactants: $\text{Mg} + \text{HCl}$. Check: Mg > H? Yes. Products: $\text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$ (Mg is $+2$, Cl is $-1$, H is diatomic). Unbalanced: $\text{Mg} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. Balance Cl and H: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$.

Reactants: $\text{Al} + \text{CuSO}_4$. Check: Al > Cu? Yes. Products: $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{Cu}$ (Al is $+3$, $\text{SO}_4$ is $-2$). Unbalanced: $\text{Al} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{Cu}$. Balance: $2\text{Al} + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{Cu}$.

Reactants: $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{KBr}$. Check: Cl > Br? Yes (Cl higher in Group 17). Products: $\text{KCl} + \text{Br}_2$ (halogens diatomic). Unbalanced: $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{KBr} \rightarrow \text{KCl} + \text{Br}_2$. Balance: $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{KBr} \rightarrow 2\text{KCl} + \text{Br}_2$.

Reactants: $\text{Fe} + \text{AgNO}_3$. Check: Fe > Ag? Yes. Products: $\text{Fe}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + \text{Ag}$ (Fe commonly $+2$ with $\text{Ag}^+$). Unbalanced: $\text{Fe} + \text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + \text{Ag}$. Balance: $\text{Fe} + 2\text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{Ag}$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. (b) $2\text{Al} + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{Cu}$. (c) $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{KBr} \rightarrow 2\text{KCl} + \text{Br}_2$. (d) $\text{Fe} + 2\text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{Ag}$. Key: check activity series first, write correct product formulas, then balance with coefficients.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing and balancing single displacement equations with correct product formulas and stoichiometry

worked_examples.title: Advanced Balancing: Transition Metal Oxidation States
worked_examples.problem: Write and balance: (a) iron + copper(II) sulfate (Fe forms $\text{Fe}^{2+}$), (b) iron + copper(II) sulfate (Fe forms $\text{Fe}^{3+}$). Explain the difference.

step_by_step.calculation: Reactants: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4$. Fe forms $\text{Fe}^{2+}$. Products: $\text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$. Balance: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$ (already balanced, 1:1:1:1).

Reactants: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4$. Fe forms $\text{Fe}^{3+}$. Products: $\text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{Cu}$. Unbalanced: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{Cu}$. Balance: $2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{Cu}$.

Fe has multiple oxidation states ($+2$, $+3$). With $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ (mild oxidizer), Fe typically forms $\text{Fe}^{2+}$. With strong oxidizers (e.g., $\text{HNO}_3$), Fe forms $\text{Fe}^{3+}$. Context determines oxidation state. $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ is more common in single displacement.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$ (Fe$^{2+}$, common). (b) $2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{Cu}$ (Fe$^{3+}$, less common). Difference: Fe has variable oxidation states; $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ is more common in single displacement with $\text{Cu}^{2+}$. Transition metals require attention to oxidation state.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Handling transition metal oxidation states in single displacement equation writing

sections.section_title: Real-World Applications of Single Displacement Reactions
sections.introduction: Single displacement reactions power countless natural processes and industrial applications. Understanding these reactions enables us to harness chemical transformations for technology, corrosion prevention, and resource recovery.

key_concept.definition: Single displacement reactions are essential in industry (metal extraction, galvanization), technology (batteries, welding), and daily life (corrosion prevention, water treatment).
key_concept.context: From protecting ships from rust to extracting valuable metals from waste, single displacement reactions have practical applications that impact economy, environment, and technology. Recognizing these applications connects classroom chemistry to real-world impact.

image.alt: Collage showing single displacement applications in industry, technology, and daily life
image.caption: Single displacement in action: galvanization, metal recovery, thermite welding, batteries.

classifications.value: Using single displacement to protect metals from corrosion
classifications.behavior: A more reactive metal (Zn, Mg, Al) is attached to or coated on a less reactive metal (Fe, steel). The reactive metal corrodes first, protecting the structural metal. This is sacrificial protection based on activity series.

examples.explanation: Steel coated with Zn layer. Zn is more reactive than Fe, so Zn corrodes first: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$. Fe is protected even if coating is scratched. Used in roofing, fencing, automotive parts. Extends steel lifespan by decades.

image.alt: Galvanized steel roofing and fencing
image.caption: Galvanized steel: Zn coating sacrificially protects Fe from corrosion.

examples.explanation: Zn or Mg blocks attached to ship hulls (steel). Zn/Mg corrodes preferentially: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$. Hull protected from seawater corrosion. Anodes replaced periodically. Saves millions in hull repair costs annually.

image.alt: Sacrificial anodes on ship hull
image.caption: Sacrificial anodes: Zn/Mg blocks protect steel ship hulls from corrosion.

examples.explanation: Mg anodes buried with steel pipelines. Mg corrodes preferentially, protecting pipeline from soil corrosion. Connected by wire for electron flow. Critical for oil/gas pipeline integrity. Prevents leaks and environmental damage.

image.alt: Sacrificial anode protecting underground pipeline
image.caption: Pipeline protection: Mg anodes protect steel pipelines from soil corrosion.

classifications.value: Using single displacement to recover valuable metals from waste
characteristics: Uses cheap scrap metal as displacing agent
classifications.behavior: Valuable metals (Cu, Ag, Au) in waste streams are recovered by adding a more reactive, cheaper metal (Fe, Zn). The valuable metal precipitates as solid, easily collected and refined.

examples.explanation: $\text{Fe}(s) + \text{CuSO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$. Scrap iron added to copper-containing wastewater. Cu precipitates as solid, collected and refined. Fe is cheap; Cu is valuable. Reduces mining demand, recovers value from waste.

image.alt: Copper recovery from solution using iron
image.caption: Copper recovery: scrap Fe displaces Cu from solution, Cu collected as solid.

examples.explanation: $\text{Zn}(s) + 2\text{AgNO}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{Zn}(\text{NO}_3)_2(aq) + 2\text{Ag}(s)$. Zn added to photographic waste containing $\text{Ag}^+$. Ag precipitates, refined and reused. Reduces Ag waste, recovers valuable metal. Important for environmental compliance.

image.alt: Silver recovery from photographic waste
image.caption: Silver recovery: Zn displaces Ag from photographic waste, Ag refined and reused.

examples.explanation: E-waste contains Au from circuit boards. Dissolved Au ($\text{Au}^{3+}$) recovered by adding Zn or Fe: $3\text{Zn} + 2\text{Au}^{3+} \rightarrow 3\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{Au}$. Au precipitates, refined. Critical for e-waste recycling, reduces mining.

image.alt: Gold recovery from electronic waste
image.caption: Gold recovery: Zn/Fe displaces Au from e-waste solution, Au refined.

classifications.value: Using highly exothermic displacement reactions for welding
characteristics: Used for rail tracks, heavy equipment
classifications.behavior: Aluminum displaces less reactive metals from their oxides in highly exothermic reactions. Temperature reaches 2500-3000°C, melting the displaced metal. Molten metal fills gaps for welding.

examples.explanation: $2\text{Al}(s) + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3(s) \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3(s) + 2\text{Fe}(l) + \text{energy}$. Al displaces Fe from oxide. Reaction reaches 2500°C, melts Fe. Molten Fe fills gap between rail ends. Creates strong, continuous rail joint. Used worldwide for rail maintenance.

image.alt: Thermite welding of railroad tracks
image.caption: Thermite welding: $2\text{Al} + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Fe}$ (molten); joins rail tracks.

examples.explanation: Thermite reaction used to cut through thick steel (emergency situations, demolition). Intense heat melts steel. Portable, no power source needed. Used by firefighters, military, demolition crews. Single displacement enables extreme heat generation.

image.alt: Thermite cutting through steel
image.caption: Thermite cutting: exothermic displacement melts steel for cutting.

classifications.value: Using displacement reactions to remove contaminants from water
classifications.behavior: More reactive metals (Fe, Zn) added to contaminated water displace toxic heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd) from solution. Toxic metals precipitate as solids, removed by filtration. Cleans wastewater before discharge.

examples.explanation: $\text{Fe}(s) + \text{Pb}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Pb}(s)$. Fe added to Pb-contaminated water. Pb precipitates as solid, filtered out. Fe is non-toxic, Pb is removed. Used in wastewater treatment, remediation of contaminated sites.

image.alt: Lead removal from contaminated water
image.caption: Lead removal: Fe displaces Pb$^{2+}$ from water, Pb filtered out as solid.

examples.explanation: $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{Hg}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Hg}(l)$. Zn added to Hg-contaminated waste. Hg precipitates as liquid metal, collected. Prevents Hg release to environment. Critical for mining, battery manufacturing wastewater treatment.

image.alt: Mercury removal from industrial wastewater
image.caption: Mercury removal: Zn displaces Hg$^{2+}$ from wastewater, Hg collected safely.

principles.statement: Single displacement applications provide economic benefits by recovering valuable materials, extending equipment lifespan, and reducing waste disposal costs.
principles.application: Copper recovery: scrap Fe (\$0.10/kg) recovers Cu (\$8/kg). Galvanization: extends steel lifespan 50+ years, reduces replacement costs. Sacrificial anodes: prevent million-dollar hull repairs.
principles.example: Electronics recycling: 1 ton of e-waste contains more Au than 17 tons of gold ore. Displacement recovery makes e-waste recycling economically viable. Reduces mining, environmental impact.

image.alt: Economic comparison of metal recovery vs. mining
image.caption: Economic benefits: displacement recovery cheaper than mining, reduces waste costs.

principles.statement: Single displacement applications reduce environmental impact by recovering metals from waste, preventing toxic metal release, and reducing mining demand.
principles.application: Metal recovery from wastewater prevents toxic metal discharge. E-waste recycling reduces mining. Galvanization reduces steel replacement frequency. All contribute to sustainability.
principles.example: Cu recovery from mine wastewater: prevents Cu toxicity in rivers, recovers valuable metal. E-waste Au recovery: reduces need for cyanide leaching in mining. Displacement reactions enable circular economy.

image.alt: Environmental benefits of metal recovery and corrosion prevention
image.caption: Environmental benefits: metal recovery prevents pollution, reduces mining impact.

components.interpretation: Effective displacement applications balance reaction feasibility (activity series), economic value (metal prices), and environmental benefit (pollution prevention, resource recovery).

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Displacement Applications
worked_examples.problem: For each application, identify: (a) the displacement reaction, (b) why it works (activity series), (c) economic benefit, (d) environmental benefit: (1) galvanized steel, (2) copper recovery from waste, (3) thermite welding.

step_by_step.calculation: (a) Reaction: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$ (Zn corrodes, protects Fe). (b) Zn > Fe on activity series, Zn corrodes preferentially. (c) Economic: extends steel lifespan 50+ years, reduces replacement costs. (d) Environmental: reduces steel production demand, less mining.

(a) Reaction: $\text{Fe} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$. (b) Fe > Cu on activity series, Fe displaces Cu. (c) Economic: scrap Fe (\$0.10/kg) recovers Cu (\$8/kg), profitable. (d) Environmental: prevents Cu discharge to waterways, reduces Cu mining demand.

(a) Reaction: $2\text{Al} + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Fe}$. (b) Al > Fe on activity series, Al displaces Fe. (c) Economic: no external power needed, portable, fast rail repair. (d) Environmental: enables rail maintenance, supports efficient transport (lower emissions than trucks).

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Galvanized steel: Zn > Fe, Zn corrodes first; extends steel life 50+ years; reduces steel production. (2) Cu recovery: Fe > Cu, Fe displaces Cu; cheap Fe recovers expensive Cu; prevents Cu pollution. (3) Thermite welding: Al > Fe, Al displaces Fe; portable welding; supports efficient rail transport. Single displacement enables practical, economic, environmental benefits.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Connecting single displacement reaction chemistry to economic and environmental applications

sections.section_title: Common Errors in Single Displacement Problems
sections.introduction: Students often make predictable errors when predicting products, checking activity series, or balancing single displacement equations. Recognizing these pitfalls and learning correction strategies builds accuracy and confidence.

key_concept.definition: Common single displacement errors include: not checking activity series, writing wrong product formulas, forgetting diatomic elements, unbalanced equations, and confusing reaction types.
key_concept.context: A verification checklist prevents errors: activity series checked? products correct? diatomic elements written correctly? equation balanced? reaction type confirmed?

image.alt: Checklist for verifying single displacement reaction solutions
image.caption: Error prevention: check activity series, products, diatomic elements, balance, reaction type.

classifications.value: Assuming reaction occurs without verifying element reactivity
characteristics: Assuming all element + compound combinations react
classifications.behavior: Results in impossible reactions (e.g., $\text{Cu} + \text{ZnSO}_4 \rightarrow$ products). Always check activity series FIRST before writing products. If free element is lower (less reactive), write 'no reaction'.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Cu} + \text{ZnSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{CuSO}_4 + \text{Zn}$. Correct: no reaction. Cu is below Zn on activity series (Cu < Zn), cannot displace Zn. Always check activity series first.

image.alt: Activity series showing Cu below Zn
image.caption: Activity series error: Cu < Zn, no reaction occurs. Check series before writing products.

examples.process: Error: assuming Ag reacts with acid
examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Ag} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{AgCl} + \text{H}_2$. Correct: no reaction. Ag is below H on activity series (Ag < H), cannot displace H from acid. Noble metals don't react with acids.

image.alt: Activity series showing Ag below H
image.caption: Activity series error: Ag < H, no reaction with acids. Noble metals are unreactive.

classifications.value: Writing incorrect formulas for products due to charge errors or element confusion
characteristics: Wrong ionic charges in product compounds
Writing element instead of compound or vice versa
classifications.behavior: Results in chemically impossible formulas. Verify product formulas: ionic compounds must have charge balance; displaced elements are often diatomic ($\text{H}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$, etc.).

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}_2$. Correct: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$. Cu is monatomic solid, not diatomic. Only H, N, O, F, Cl, Br, I are diatomic as elements.

image.alt: Correct vs. incorrect copper formula
image.caption: Formula error: Cu is monatomic solid, not Cu₂. Diatomic elements: HOFBrINCl.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}$. Correct: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. Hydrogen is diatomic ($\text{H}_2$) as element. Always write $\text{H}_2$, not H, for hydrogen gas.

image.alt: Correct vs. incorrect hydrogen formula
image.caption: Hydrogen error: H₂ (diatomic), not H. Seven diatomic elements: HOFBrINCl.

classifications.value: Writing correct products but failing to balance the equation
characteristics: Atoms not equal on both sides
Violates Law of Conservation of Mass
Forgetting to balance after predicting products
classifications.behavior: Unbalanced equations misrepresent reaction stoichiometry; cannot use for calculations. Always verify atom counts after writing products. Balance polyatomic ions as units when possible.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Al} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{Cu}$. Left: 1 Al, 1 Cu, 1 $\text{SO}_4$; Right: 2 Al, 1 Cu, 3 $\text{SO}_4$. Correct: $2\text{Al} + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{Cu}$. Balance Al and $\text{SO}_4$.

image.alt: Balancing Al + CuSO₄ equation
image.caption: Balancing: $2\text{Al} + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{Cu}$; equal atoms both sides.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{NaBr} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{Br}_2$. Left: 2 Cl, 1 Na, 1 Br; Right: 1 Cl, 1 Na, 2 Br. Correct: $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{NaBr} \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{Br}_2$. Balance Cl and Br.

image.alt: Balancing Cl₂ + NaBr equation
image.caption: Balancing: $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{NaBr} \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{Br}_2$; 2 Cl, 2 Na, 2 Br both sides.

classifications.value: Misidentifying single displacement as synthesis, decomposition, or double replacement
characteristics: Assuming element + compound is always single displacement
Not recognizing pattern $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$
Confusing with double replacement ($AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$)
classifications.behavior: Leads to wrong product predictions. Always check pattern: single displacement has ONE element + ONE compound → ONE element + ONE compound. Two elements = synthesis. One compound = decomposition.

examples.process: Error: confusing single with double displacement
examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4$ called double displacement. Correct: This is single displacement (element + compound → element + compound). Double displacement requires TWO compounds: $\text{AB} + \text{CD} \rightarrow \text{AD} + \text{CB}$.

image.alt: Comparison of single vs. double displacement patterns
image.caption: Single displacement: element + compound → element + compound. Double: compound + compound → compound + compound.

examples.process: Error: confusing single displacement with synthesis
examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeCuSO}_4$ called synthesis. Correct: This is single displacement: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$. Synthesis is element + element → compound ($A + B \rightarrow AB$).

image.alt: Comparison of single displacement vs. synthesis patterns
image.caption: Single displacement: $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$. Synthesis: $A + B \rightarrow AB$. Different patterns.

principles.statement: Always verify single displacement solutions by checking: (1) Activity series checked (reaction occurs?), (2) Correct products (element replaced correctly?), (3) Diatomic elements written correctly ($\text{H}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{Br}_2$, $\text{I}_2$), (4) Equation balanced (atoms equal), (5) Ionic formulas correct (charge balance).
principles.application: Prevents common errors; systematic verification builds accuracy habit. Use checklist for every single displacement problem.
principles.example: Problem: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow$. Solution: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$. Check: Activity series (Zn > Cu) ✓, Products correct (Zn replaces Cu) ✓, Diatomic (Cu is monatomic) ✓, Balanced (1 Zn, 1 Cu, 1 $\text{SO}_4$ both sides) ✓, Ionic formulas ($\text{Zn}^{2+}$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) ✓.

image.alt: Checklist for verifying single displacement reaction solutions
image.caption: Verification: activity series, products, diatomic elements, balance, ionic formulas.

principles.statement: Seven elements are diatomic in elemental form: $\text{H}_2$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{O}_2$, $\text{F}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{Br}_2$, $\text{I}_2$; always write them as diatomic when they appear as products in single displacement reactions.
principles.application: Prevents formula errors: write $\text{H}_2$ not H, $\text{Cl}_2$ not Cl, $\text{Br}_2$ not Br. Mnemonic: HOFBrINCl (pronounced 'Hoff-brin-kul') or 'Have No Fear Of Ice Cold Beer'.

image.alt: List of diatomic elements with mnemonic
image.caption: Diatomic elements: $\text{H}_2$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{O}_2$, $\text{F}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{Br}_2$, $\text{I}_2$; always write as diatomic in reactions.

worked_examples.title: Error Identification and Correction
worked_examples.problem: Identify the error in each single displacement attempt and provide the correct solution: (a) $\text{Cu} + \text{ZnSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{CuSO}_4 + \text{Zn}$, (b) $\text{Mg} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl} + \text{H}$, (c) $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{NaBr} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{Br}_2$ (unbalanced).

step_by_step.calculation: Error: didn't check activity series. Cu is below Zn (Cu < Zn), cannot displace Zn. Correct: no reaction. Activity series must be checked FIRST before writing products.

Two errors: (1) Wrong ionic formula ($\text{MgCl}$ should be $\text{MgCl}_2$, Mg is $+2$, Cl is $-1$). (2) H should be diatomic ($\text{H}_2$). Correct: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$.

Error: unbalanced. Products correct (Cl replaces Br), but atoms unequal. Left: 2 Cl, 1 Na, 1 Br; Right: 1 Cl, 1 Na, 2 Br. Correct: $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{NaBr} \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{Br}_2$. Balance Cl and Br.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Error: didn't check activity series. Correction: no reaction (Cu < Zn, cannot displace). (b) Errors: wrong formula ($\text{MgCl}_2$ not $\text{MgCl}$), H should be $\text{H}_2$. Correction: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. (c) Error: unbalanced. Correction: $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{NaBr} \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{Br}_2$. Verification checklist prevents these errors.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying and correcting common single displacement reaction errors

sections.introduction: Single displacement reaction mastery requires active practice with activity series application, product prediction, equation balancing, and real-world application. Work through these problems to build confidence and accuracy.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems cover: predicting if reactions occur using activity series, writing correct product formulas, balancing equations, identifying reaction types, and connecting reactions to applications.
key_concept.context: Active recall and immediate feedback reinforce activity series memorization and pattern recognition; focus on checking activity series first, writing correct formulas, and verification steps.

image.alt: Practice strategy flowchart for single displacement reactions
image.caption: Practice strategy: check activity series → predict products → write formulas → balance → verify.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Single Displacement Practice Set
worked_examples.problem: Predict products and balance (write 'no reaction' if none): (a) $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow$, (b) $\text{Cu} + \text{FeSO}_4 \rightarrow$, (c) $\text{Zn} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow$, (d) $\text{Ag} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow$, (e) $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{KI} \rightarrow$, (f) $\text{Br}_2 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow$.

step_by_step.calculation: Check activity series: Fe > Cu? Yes. Reaction occurs. Products: $\text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$ (Fe forms $\text{Fe}^{2+}$). Balance: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$ (already balanced).
Check activity series: Cu > Fe? No (Cu < Fe). Reaction does NOT occur. Answer: no reaction.
Check activity series: Zn > H? Yes. Reaction occurs. Products: $\text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. Balance: $\text{Zn} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$.
Check activity series: Ag > H? No (Ag < H). Reaction does NOT occur. Answer: no reaction.
Check activity series (Group 17): Cl > Br > I. Cl > I? Yes. Reaction occurs. Products: $\text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$. Balance: $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{KI} \rightarrow 2\text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$.
Check activity series (Group 17): Cl > Br. Br > Cl? No. Reaction does NOT occur. Answer: no reaction.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$. (b) no reaction (Cu < Fe). (c) $\text{Zn} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. (d) no reaction (Ag < H). (e) $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{KI} \rightarrow 2\text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$. (f) no reaction (Br < Cl). Key: always check activity series FIRST to determine if reaction occurs.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive single displacement practice: activity series application, product prediction, balancing, no reaction cases

worked_examples.title: Single Displacement Reaction Identification Challenge
worked_examples.problem: Identify which are single displacement reactions and predict/balance products: (a) $\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow$, (b) $\text{Zn} + \text{CuCl}_2 \rightarrow$, (c) $\text{NaCl} + \text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow$, (d) $\text{Al} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow$, (e) $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow$.

step_by_step.calculation: Not single displacement: TWO elements (Mg + $\text{O}_2$). This is synthesis: $2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO}$. Pattern $A + B \rightarrow AB$, not $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$.
Single displacement: element (Zn) + compound ($\text{CuCl}_2$). Check: Zn > Cu? Yes. Products: $\text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{Cu}$. Balance: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuCl}_2 \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{Cu}$.

Not single displacement: TWO compounds ($\text{NaCl} + \text{AgNO}_3$). This is double replacement: $\text{NaCl} + \text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{NaNO}_3 + \text{AgCl}\downarrow$. Pattern $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$.

Single displacement: element (Al) + compound ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$). Check: Al > H? Yes. Products: $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{H}_2$. Balance: $2\text{Al} + 3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{H}_2$.

Not single displacement: ONE reactant ($\text{CaCO}_3$). This is decomposition: $\text{CaCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$. Pattern $AB \rightarrow A + B$, not $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$.

worked_examples.final_answer: Single displacement reactions: (b) $\text{Zn} + \text{CuCl}_2 \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{Cu}$, (d) $2\text{Al} + 3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{H}_2$. Not single displacement: (a) synthesis (two elements), (c) double replacement (two compounds), (e) decomposition (one reactant). Key: single displacement has ONE element + ONE compound → ONE element + ONE compound.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying single displacement reactions among mixed reaction types

summary: Single displacement reactions follow $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$: more reactive element (A) replaces less reactive element (B) in compound. Activity series determines if reaction occurs: metals higher can displace metals lower; metals above H react with acids. Types: (1) Metal displacing metal: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$ (Zn > Cu); (2) Metal displacing H: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$ (Mg > H); (3) Halogen displacing halogen: $\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{KBr} \rightarrow 2\text{KCl} + \text{Br}_2$ (Cl > Br). Activity series: Li > K > Ca > Na > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Ni > Sn > Pb > H > Cu > Ag > Au. Redox reactions: displacing element oxidized (loses e⁻), displaced element reduced (gains e⁻). Applications: corrosion prevention (galvanization, sacrificial anodes), metal recovery (Cu from waste), thermite welding ($2\text{Al} + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Fe}$), water treatment (heavy metal removal). Common errors: not checking activity series (write 'no reaction' if A < B), wrong product formulas (balance charges, diatomic elements $\text{H}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{Br}_2$, $\text{I}_2$), unbalanced equations. Verification checklist: activity series checked? products correct? diatomic correct? balanced? ionic formulas correct? Mastery enables prediction of reaction outcomes, understanding of corrosion, and recognition of single displacement in industrial and environmental applications.
